mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z4 2.5i or 2.5si engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Z4 2.5i/2.5si engine typically costs £1,800–£2,700 supply only depending on which generation (M54 or N52), with used units available from £1,300–£1,900. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW Z4 2.5i / 2.5si engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — both the M54 and N52 are among BMW's most reliable engines. The N52 is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable engines. The M54 has well-known VANOS seal and cooling system weaknesses, but these are well-understood and repairable. With good maintenance, both engines are capable of well over 200,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW Z4 2.5i and 2.5si?

acceptedAnswer.text: M54: VANOS seal degradation (loss of low-end torque), cooling system failures (water pump, expansion tank), CCV system issues. N52: Valvetronic sensor failure (rough idle, stalling), oil leaks (valve cover gasket, oil filter housing), cooling system failures. Both engines are fundamentally robust.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW Z4 2.5i / 2.5si engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Z4 values remain strong — a well-kept example is often worth £4,000–£12,000+. A £1,800–£2,700 reconditioned engine is only 15–40% of the vehicle's value — within the 70% BMW threshold, but evaluate carefully. On a cherished example, replacement is almost always worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW Z4 2.5i or 2.5si engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. E85/E86 Z4 2.5i (2003–2005) uses M54B25; E85/E86 Z4 2.5i/2.5si (2005–2008) uses N52B25. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M54 VANOS seals, or does the system need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The VANOS seals can be replaced with upgraded Viton/Teflon seals (OEM kit 11367841015). Replacement costs £400–£800 and restores throttle response and low-end torque. The system itself does not need replacing — just the seals. This is well-understood by BMW specialists.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Z4 2.5i/2.5si market. M54-engined cars sit at the lower end of the range; N52 engines command a slight premium. Used M54B25 engines typically cost £1,300–£1,900; reconditioned units range from £2,000–£2,700. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Z4 2.5i/2.5si engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The engine bay is tight, making it a specialist job.

problems.title: VANOS Seal Degradation (M54B25)
problems.symptoms: Loss of low-end torque, hesitation on acceleration, rough idle, reduced fuel economy, rattling noise from the front of the engine
problems.repairCost: £400–£800 (VANOS seal replacement with upgraded Viton/Teflon seals)
problems.recommendation: The M54's VANOS system is a common weak point. The original seals degrade over time, causing loss of low-end torque and hesitation. Upgraded Viton/Teflon seals (OEM kit 11367841015) are available and restore throttle response. Replacement is well-understood by BMW specialists. If you notice hesitation or rough idle on an M54-engined Z4, have the VANOS system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full VANOS failure guide

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (M54 and N52)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, rising temperature gauge, warning light, steam from the engine bay, heater not working properly
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (water pump, thermostat, or expansion tank replacement)
problems.recommendation: Both the M54 and N52 suffer from cooling system weaknesses. The water pump, thermostat, and expansion tank are known failure points. On the M54, the expansion tank (OEM 17117573781) and water pump (11517527910) should be replaced preventatively. Overheating is the leading cause of engine damage on these engines. Check coolant levels regularly and investigate any drop immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: Valvetronic Sensor and Oil Leaks (N52B25)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, stalling, hesitation, poor throttle response, oil spots under the car, burning oil smell
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (Valvetronic sensor replacement or valve cover gasket)
problems.recommendation: The N52's Valvetronic system can suffer from sensor failure, causing rough idle and stalling. Oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing are also common on the N52. If you notice rough idle or oil spots, have the Valvetronic system and gaskets inspected. Catch oil leaks early to prevent low oil pressure and potential engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full Valvetronic failure guide

Well-understood job — catch before oil starvation

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Z4 2.5i / 2.5si? Check These First
buyIf: * N52-engined car (2005–2008) — the more reliable and refined engine
* M54-engined car with documented VANOS seal and cooling system work — the defining maintenance on the early car
* Full service history with regular oil changes — both engines reward good maintenance
* Evidence of cooling system maintenance — water pump, thermostat, and expansion tank replaced
avoidIf: * M54 with no proof of VANOS work — budget for it immediately
* Any signs of overheating or coolant loss — cooling system issues are expensive
* N52 with rough idle or stalling — Valvetronic sensor issues are likely
* Poor service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Z4 2.5i and 2.5si are powered by two of BMW's finest naturally aspirated straight-sixes. The M54 (2003–2005) is smooth and characterful but suffers from VANOS seal degradation and cooling system weaknesses. The N52 (2005–2008) is more refined and reliable, with Valvetronic and double-VANOS, but can suffer from sensor and oil leak issues. Both engines are well-understood by specialists and reward diligent maintenance with exceptional longevity. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the Z4 will reward you with one of the best driving experiences of any roadster.

cta.label: → Compare Z4 2.5i / 2.5si engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW Z4 2.5i or 2.5si (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. VANOS Performance (M54) — Any hesitation or loss of low-end torque? (VANOS seal warning)
2. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (cooling system warning on both engines)
3. Service History — including which generation (M54 or N52) and evidence of major maintenance
4. Idle Quality (N52) — Any rough idle or stalling? (Valvetronic sensor warning)
5. Oil Spots — Any oil under the car? (valve cover or oil filter housing leak warning)
6. Cold Start — Any rattles? (timing chain issues are rare but possible)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — two engine families across the Z4 2.5i/2.5si's history.
